g.data-narration: Welcome to Storage Attachment Methods. We'll explore how computers connect to storage devices and the trade-offs between different approaches.
g.text: Storage Attachment Methods - DAS vs NAS vs SAN

g.data-narration: Computers access secondary storage in three main ways: directly attached, over a network, or through a dedicated storage network. Each has different performance characteristics.
g.text: The way computers connect to storage affects
• Scalability - ability to add more storage

g.data-narration: There are three primary storage attachment methods: Direct Attached Storage for maximum performance, Network Attached Storage for easy sharing, and Storage Area Networks for enterprise environments.
Host-Attached Storage connects directly via local I/O ports. The storage device uses a hardware interface like SATA or SAS to communicate with the host computer.
g.text: Storage directly connected to the host computer through local I/O ports.
The host CPU sends I/O commands via device driver; data transfers via DMA (Direct Memory Access).

g.data-narration: Common DAS interfaces include SATA for desktops, SAS for enterprise servers, and NVMe for ultra-fast SSDs using the PCIe bus directly.
DAS offers the fastest access with lowest latency but is limited to a single host and has scalability constraints due to available I/O ports.
Network-Attached Storage provides file-level storage over a standard data network. It's essentially a specialized computer that serves files to multiple clients.
g.text: File-level storage accessed over a standard data network (LAN/WAN) using TCP/IP.
A NAS device runs a file system and serves files to client machines over the network.

g.data-narration: NAS architecture shows clients connecting to a NAS device over a network. The NAS device contains its own file system and RAID storage, serving files to multiple simultaneous clients.
NAS uses file-level protocols: NFS for Unix/Linux systems, and CIFS/SMB for Windows and cross-platform environments.
A Storage Area Network is a dedicated high-speed network connecting servers to shared storage. Unlike NAS, SAN provides block-level access - servers see storage as local disks.
g.text: A dedicated, high-speed network connecting servers to shared storage devices.
Provides block-level access - servers see SAN storage as locally attached disks.

g.data-narration: SAN architecture uses a fabric of switches connecting multiple servers to various storage devices including disk arrays, tape libraries, and SSD arrays.
SAN uses block-level protocols: Fibre Channel for highest performance, iSCSI for cost-effective solutions over standard Ethernet, and FCoE combining both technologies.

Here's a comprehensive comparison: DAS offers highest performance at lowest cost but limited sharing. NAS provides easy file sharing. SAN delivers high performance with excellent scalability for enterprise databases.
g.text: Comparison: DAS vs NAS vs SAN

g.data-narration: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) combines multiple disks to improve performance and reliability. Different RAID levels offer different trade-offs between speed, capacity, and fault tolerance.
g.text: RAID - Redundant Array of Independent Disks
RAID uses multiple disks together to improve performance and reliability.
Different levels offer trade-offs: higher performance vs. more redundancy vs. usable capacity.

g.data-narration: RAID 0 stripes for performance but has no redundancy. RAID 1 mirrors data for safety. RAID 5 uses distributed parity. RAID 6 adds double parity. RAID 10 combines mirroring and striping.
RAID 0 stripes data across disks for parallel I/O. RAID 1 mirrors all data to both disks. RAID 5 distributes parity across all disks to reconstruct data after a failure.
RAID 6 has double parity to survive two disk failures. RAID 10 mirrors first then stripes, providing excellent performance and fault tolerance with 50% capacity overhead.
In summary: Choose DAS for single workstations, NAS for file sharing, SAN for enterprise databases. Use RAID levels based on your need for speed versus redundancy.
g.text: • DAS: Fastest, direct connection, single host
• NAS: File-level access over network, easy sharing
